from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Triple:
    subject: str
    predicate: str
    object: str

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-START

def predict(triples: list[Triple]) -> str:
    """
    Convert a list of triples about universities (or related entities) into a single
    fluent sentence. Each predicate is mapped to a short verbal phrase; the resulting
    clauses are concatenated with commas.
    """
    if not triples:
        return ""

    # Simple predicate → phrase mapping for natural language rendering
    phrase_map = {
        "city": "is located in the city",
        "country": "is located in the country",
        "latinName": "has the Latin name",
        "nickname": "is nicknamed",
        "rector": "has rector",
        "academicStaffSize": "has an academic staff size of",
        "established": "was established in",
        "state": "is in the state",
        "governmentType": "has government type",
        "hasToItsNortheast": "has to its northeast",
        "dean": "has dean",
        "location": "is located at",
        "numberOfStudents": "has number of students",
        "affiliation": "is affiliated with",
        "director": "has director",
        "motto": "has motto",
        "numberOfPostgraduateStudents": "has number of postgraduate students",
        "numberOfUndergraduateStudents": "has number of undergraduate students",
        "officialSchoolColour": "has official school colour",
        "outlookRanking": "has outlook ranking",
        "sportsOffered": "offers sport",
        "wasGivenTheTechnicalCampusStatusBy": "was given the technical campus status by",
        "founder": "was founded by",
        "leader": "has leader",
        "leaderTitle": "has leader title",
        "religion": "has religion",
        "headquarter": "has headquarters in",
        "largestCity": "has largest city",
        "river": "has river",
        "hasToItsWest": "has to its west",
        "anthem": "has anthem",
        "ethnicGroup": "has ethnic group",
        "legislature": "has legislature",
        "sportGoverningBody": "has sport governing body",
        "isPartOf": "is part of",
        "neighboringMunicipality": "has neighboring municipality",
        "capital": "has capital",
        "patronSaint": "has patron saint",
        "hasToItsNorthwest": "has to its northwest",
        "campus": "has campus",
        "president": "has president",
        "staff": "has staff",
        "numberOfDoctoralStudents": "has number of doctoral students",
        "elevationAboveTheSeaLevel": "has elevation above sea level",
        "postalCode": "has postal code",
        "longName": "has long name",
    }

    # Group triples by subject to enable richer, single‑sentence rendering per entity
    subject_map = {}
    for t in triples:
        subject_map.setdefault(t.subject, []).append((t.predicate, t.object))

    sentences = []
    for subject, preds in subject_map.items():
        parts = []
        for pred, obj in preds:
            verb_phrase = phrase_map.get(pred, pred)
            # Keep original quoting; add quotes only if the object looks like a plain phrase with spaces
            if not (obj.startswith('"') and obj.endswith('"')) and " " in obj:
                obj = f'"{obj}"'
            parts.append(f"{verb_phrase} {obj}")

        # Build a fluent clause for this subject
        if len(parts) == 1:
            clause = f"{subject} {parts[0]}"
        else:
            # commas between all but the last, and 'and' before the final part
            clause = f"{subject} " + ", ".join(parts[:-1]) + f", and {parts[-1]}"
        sentences.append(clause)

    # Combine subject clauses into one (or more) sentences
    final_text = " ".join(sentences) + "."
    # Capitalize the first character of the whole output
    return final_text[0].upper() + final_text[1:]

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-END

You will be given the current Python program with its BLUE score and an inspiration program and your task will be to evolve the current Python program to improve the score. It is a program that converts a list of triples into natural language text. The given triples will be associated with the topic: University. The program must implement a function called 'predict' that accepts a list of triples and generates a coherent, contextually relevant sentence that accurately represents the information contained in the triples. Ensure that the generated text is fluent, grammatically correct, and maintains the meaning of the original data. There can be multiple triples given that make up a complex sentence. Converting all those triples into one sentence will award greater score. An example of a complex sentence:
Triples:
(Antwerp | cityServed | Antwerp International Airport)
(Belgium | country | Antwerp)
(City of Brussels | capital | Belgium)
Example sentence:
"Antwerp International Airport serves the city of Antwerp which is in Belgium, where the capital is Brussels."

The 'predict' function returns that sentence as a string. Below is the list of all possible predicates that can be given as an input. With the given predicates and example triples implement the 'predict' function so it can process all the possible predicates:

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Triple:
    subject: str
    predicate: str
    object: str

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-START

def predict(triples: list[Triple]) -> str:
    """
    Convert a list of triples about universities (or related entities) into a single
    fluent sentence. Each predicate is mapped to a short verbal phrase; the resulting
    clauses are concatenated with commas.
    """
    if not triples:
        return ""

    # Simple predicate → phrase mapping for natural language rendering
    phrase_map = {
        "city": "is located in the city",
        "country": "is located in the country",
        "latinName": "has the Latin name",
        "nickname": "is nicknamed",
        "rector": "has rector",
        "academicStaffSize": "has an academic staff size of",
        "established": "was established in",
        "state": "is in the state",
        "governmentType": "has government type",
        "hasToItsNortheast": "has to its northeast",
        "dean": "has dean",
        "location": "is located at",
        "numberOfStudents": "has number of students",
        "affiliation": "is affiliated with",
        "director": "has director",
        "motto": "has motto",
        "numberOfPostgraduateStudents": "has number of postgraduate students",
        "numberOfUndergraduateStudents": "has number of undergraduate students",
        "officialSchoolColour": "has official school colour",
        "outlookRanking": "has outlook ranking",
        "sportsOffered": "offers sport",
        "wasGivenTheTechnicalCampusStatusBy": "was given the technical campus status by",
        "founder": "was founded by",
        "leader": "has leader",
        "leaderTitle": "has leader title",
        "religion": "has religion",
        "headquarter": "has headquarters in",
        "largestCity": "has largest city",
        "river": "has river",
        "hasToItsWest": "has to its west",
        "anthem": "has anthem",
        "ethnicGroup": "has ethnic group",
        "legislature": "has legislature",
        "sportGoverningBody": "has sport governing body",
        "isPartOf": "is part of",
        "neighboringMunicipality": "has neighboring municipality",
        "capital": "has capital",
        "patronSaint": "has patron saint",
        "hasToItsNorthwest": "has to its northwest",
        "campus": "has campus",
        "president": "has president",
        "staff": "has staff",
        "numberOfDoctoralStudents": "has number of doctoral students",
        "elevationAboveTheSeaLevel": "has elevation above sea level",
        "postalCode": "has postal code",
        "longName": "has long name",
    }

    clauses = []
    for t in triples:
        verb_phrase = phrase_map.get(t.predicate, t.predicate)
        # Ensure object is quoted only when it contains spaces and is not already quoted
        obj = t.object
        if " " in obj and not (obj.startswith('"') and obj.endswith('"')):
            obj = f'"{obj}"'
        clauses.append(f"{t.subject} {verb_phrase} {obj}")

    # Join clauses into a single sentence
    sentence = ", ".join(clauses) + "."
    # Capitalize first character
    return sentence[0].upper() + sentence[1:]

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-END
